data.title: Vistra, PG&E top picks at TD Cowen in initiating coverage of U.S. power and utility sector
data.description:
TD Cowen tapped Vistra (NYSE:VST) and PG&E (NYSE:PCG) as its Top Picks in the U.S. power and utility sector, which it initiated with a "positive bias" in anticipation of a strong need for robust infrastructure in the face of accelerated demand for electricity from data centers, as well as the need to upgrade aging utility assets, meaning that power prices will remain elevated in key deregulated markets for the foreseeable future.

TD Cowen cited a straightforward case for Vistra (NYSE:VST), whose generation assets benefit from locational advantages, particularly around the fast-growing Dallas market, and the bank expects EBITDA to grow by an average 8.4% annually through 2030, driven by tightening power supply/demand, leading to higher prices for both energy and capacity.

Vistra (VST) is expected to benefit from structural tailwinds in the power space as electricity demand continues to grow faster than new generation can come online, and TD Cowen also finds the company's ERCOT footprint compelling vs. peers given the market dynamics in Texas outpacing the rest of the country as well as lower regulatory and political risk in future years.

California's chaos has created an attractive entry point, according to TD Cowen, with PG&E (NYSE:PCG), the state's largest utility, as its other Top Pick - although Buy-rated Edison International (EIX) is not far behind - viewing the recent passage of AB 254 as a step in the right direction, as the legislation further improves financial mitigation for large wildfires while delaying when utilities need to commit more capital; to the extent PG&E can continue to harden the grid against future wildfires, the potential to contribute more capital diminishes, the analysts said.

Also initiated with Buy ratings: American Electric Power (AEP), Duke Energy (DUK), NextEra Energy (NEE), Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG), Southern (SO), Xcel Energy (XEL).

Started at Neutral: American Water Works (AWK), Eversource Energy (ES), MDU Resources (MDU), Pinnacle West (PNW), WEC Energy (WEC).

ETF: (XLU)

data.title: Jefferies, Zions, Western Alliance sink, leading regional bank stocks down on credit risk concern
data.description:
At 3:53 PM ET updates with addition of Jefferies to stocks that are declining and updates stock price movement.

Stocks of investment bank Jefferies Financial (JEF) and regional banks Zions Bancorporation (NASDAQ:ZION), and Western Alliance tanked on Thursday as investors worried about the quality of loans and investments made by the firms. That's led to weakness across regional banks. The KBW Nasdaq Regional Banking Index (KRX) sank 6.8% in late Thursday trading, its biggest drop since April, following the disclosures by two banks of irregularities related to some loans. 

Jefferies (JEF) stock slid 10%, continuing a decline started when it revealed its exposure to bankrupt auto-parts maker First Brands last week.

After Wednesday's close, Zions Bancorporation (NASDAQ:ZION) said it found "apparent misrepresentations and contractual defaults" by the borrowers and supporting collateral when it reviewed two related loans made by its California Bank & Trust division. Zions took a provision for the full $60M outstanding under the loans.

On Thursday, Western Alliance Bancorporation (NYSE:WAL) said it sued a borrower, alleging fraud in failing to provide collateral loans in first position, among other claims.

Zions (NASDAQ:ZION) stock plunged 13% and Western Alliance (NYSE:WAL) tumbled 12% in Thursday trading.

That apparently has investors wary of other regional banks. Among the biggest decliners in the sector: Flagstar Financial (NYSE:FLG) -7.3%, Banc of California (NYSE:BANC) -8.3%, Webster Financial (NYSE:WBS) -7.7%, Metropolitan Bank Holding (NYSE:MCB) -8.7%, Axos Financial (NYSE:AX) -8.5%, and Bank OZK (NASDAQ:OZK) -6/0%.

data.title: SLB N.V. Q3 Preview: Analysts see muted quarter with softer earnings
data.description:
SLB N.V. (NYSE:SLB) is projected to see a nearly 25% decline in its third-quarter earnings, which the company is due to report on October 17, before market open.

The consensus EPS Estimate is $0.67, while revenue is likely to fall 26% year-on-year to $8.92 billion. Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 17 downward revisions while revenue estimates have seen 6 upward revisions and 11 downward moves.

Analysts at RBC Capital Markets expect a neutral to slightly positive setup heading into the Q3 print. They see the company posting stable Q3 results but without any significant announcements.

“SLB stock has been devoid of near-term catalysts, which these results are unlikely to change. Segregated Digital business disclosures should be incremental, but unlikely to be needle-moving in the near-term,” analysts led by Keith Mackey said.

However, going ahead, the company’s expanding digital platform positions it to deliver stronger financial returns in a capital-disciplined oil and gas market, they said. Its Transition Technologies portfolio and expanding energy transition capabilities are also likely to support future revenue growth.

RBC issued an Outperform rating for the stock on the back of higher-than-expected commodity prices and the firm’s stronger-than-expected pricing for services, among other reasons.

However, the brokerage lowered its price target from $46 to $43 over risks associated with reduced industry activity, which it says could weigh on SLB’s results; tariff threats and government regulations or sanctions. RCB is also cautious about the capital invested in the New Energy portfolio, which it says may not realize return on capital metrics.

In the year so far, the stock has declined 14.45%, underperforming the wider markets. Over the last 2 years, SLB has beaten EPS estimates 88% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 63% of the time.

data.title: Rexford Industrial raises 2025 core FFO midpoint to $2.40 with $500M share repurchase program amid strong leasing and capital recycling
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (REXR) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Laura Clark, Chief Operating Officer, highlighted that "third quarter results, which were ahead of expectations, are a testament to the strength of our business model and our focus on driving value." She noted execution of 3.3 million square feet of leasing, nearly double last quarter, and a 60 basis point increase in same-property ending occupancy to 96.8%. Clark emphasized that Rexford's portfolio outperformed the broader infill market, with net absorption of 1.9 million square feet compared to the overall market's 400,000 square feet. She reported that "leasing spreads for comparable leases were 26% and 10% on a net effective and cash basis, respectively, and in line with expectations." Clark further discussed unlocking near-term NOI and capital recycling through strategic dispositions and noted "bad debt levels are below historical averages at 30 basis points as a percentage of revenue year-to-date." She stated, "We continue to focus on allocating capital to drive the highest risk-adjusted returns while remaining cognizant of market conditions."
  • Michael Fitzmaurice, Chief Financial Officer, reported, "Third quarter Core FFO was $0.60 per share, up $0.01 from last quarter, driven by higher occupancy and accretive capital recycling from dispositions and the share repurchases." Fitzmaurice noted the Board authorized a new $500 million share repurchase program and stated, "We are raising our full year 2025 core FFO per share midpoint to $2.40, up $0.01 compared to last quarter." He highlighted $150 million of share repurchases executed in the quarter, funded by disposition proceeds. Fitzmaurice mentioned, "We continue to allocate capital with a focus on FFO and NAV per share accretion while preserving healthy levels of liquidity totaling $1.6 billion as of quarter end and maintaining a low net debt to EBITDA of 4.1x."

Outlook

  • The company raised its full year 2025 core FFO per share midpoint to $2.40, up $0.01 from last quarter. Fitzmaurice stated, "The increase is driven by strong leasing activity, accretive capital recycling from dispositions and the share repurchases and higher capitalized interest."
  • Same-property cash NOI midpoint increased to 4%, up 150 basis points from last quarter, attributed to lower concessions within the same-property pool. Fitzmaurice also described that "projected lease-up delays related to repositioning and redevelopment projects" partially offset guidance increases.

Financial Results

  • Core FFO for the third quarter was $0.60 per share. Total portfolio occupancy, including repositioning and redevelopment, increased by 260 basis points sequentially. Notable rent commencements included 500,000 square feet at 1601 Mission, 191,000 square feet at 218 Turnbull Canyon, and 123,000 square feet at 8888 Balboa.
  • The company executed $150 million in share repurchases and completed $54 million of property dispositions during the quarter, with year-to-date dispositions reaching $188 million at a weighted average exit cap rate of 4.2%. Repositioning and redevelopment leases totaled 845,000 square feet in the quarter, contributing to $27 million of annualized incremental NOI year-to-date.
  • Fitzmaurice described $65 million of projected annualized NOI from repositioning and redevelopment as of the third quarter, with $41 million tied to projects stabilized or in lease-up and $24 million under construction. He noted an expected offset of about $25 million of annualized NOI coming offline as future projects commence construction in late 2025 and throughout 2026.

Q&A

  • Samir Khanal, BofA Securities, questioned the sustainability of the 3.3 million square feet of leasing. Clark responded that "we had a great quarter of leasing 3.3 million square feet, the highest actually leasing quarter in our history," but cautioned on macroeconomic uncertainty and its impact on future demand.
  • Michael Griffin, Evercore ISI, asked about the trade-off between occupancy and concessions or rent. Clark explained the strategy is focused on "driving occupancy, driving cash flow and NOI," sometimes favoring immediate NOI over rate.
  • Michael Mueller, JPMorgan, inquired about the disposition strategy and redevelopment pipeline. Howard Schwimmer, Co-CEO, stated ongoing evaluation of assets for sale or reinvestment, and Clark emphasized the flexibility to pause, lease as is, or sell assets for optimal value creation.
  • Craig Mailman, Citigroup, asked about share buybacks and potential influence from Elliott. Michael Frankel, Co-CEO, clarified, "we have met with Elliott, and we have a constructive dialogue," but the buyback program predated Elliott’s involvement.
  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo, questioned credit and bad debt trends. Fitzmaurice replied, "The tenant health in our portfolio has been resilient and look no further than the bad debt levels that we've experienced over the last couple of quarters. In the second quarter, we had a negligible $100,000. This quarter, it was effectively 0."
  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho, inquired about cap rates and mark-to-market. Fitzmaurice confirmed occupancy on sold assets was about 67%, and on a cash basis, mark-to-market was negative 1% for the quarter, noting possible pressure on re-leasing spreads into 2026 and 2027.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was cautious, probing the sustainability of leasing momentum, the impact of concessions on revenue growth, and the strategy around asset sales and buybacks. There was concern over future rent spreads and market demand given ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, consistently emphasizing operational flexibility, capital discipline, and multiple levers for growth. Clark stated, "We are certainly encouraged by what we're seeing in the market," while also acknowledging uncertainty.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained cautious but focused more on near-term execution and risk mitigation, while management displayed slightly increased confidence, citing improved leasing activity, higher occupancy, and strong capital recycling.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised its core FFO per share midpoint and same-property cash NOI midpoint, while last quarter guidance was reaffirmed. Leasing activity nearly doubled quarter-over-quarter, with the highest leasing figures in company history.
  • Management tone shifted from steady optimism in Q2 to more assertive confidence in Q3, citing outperformance versus the market and robust operational results. Analysts' focus shifted from concerns over lease-up timing and market rent declines in Q2 to questions about the sustainability of current performance and the impact of strategic shifts like share repurchases in Q3.
  • Strategic focus evolved further toward capital recycling, share repurchases, and portfolio optimization, with an explicit pause on acquisitions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic and tariff policy uncertainty as ongoing risks, potentially impacting tenant demand. Clark indicated, "uncertainty around the overall macroeconomic environment and tariff policy remains, which could continue to impact tenant demand in an unpredictable manner."
  • Fitzmaurice noted lease-up delays related to repositioning and redevelopment projects as a partial offset to guidance increases.
  • Analysts raised concerns over the potential for negative re-leasing spreads in 2026 and 2027 and the impact of asset sales on recurring income and dividend policy.

Final Takeaway

Rexford Industrial delivered a record quarter of leasing and raised its 2025 core FFO guidance, supported by strong operational execution, strategic dispositions, and a new $500 million share repurchase program. Management highlighted robust portfolio performance and proactive capital allocation, while remaining attentive to macroeconomic risks and maintaining operational flexibility to drive future shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Apple works to release touch-screen MacBook as soon as next year: report
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) is developing a touch-screen version of its MacBook Pro that it might release by late 2026 or early 2027, according to a report by Bloomberg.

The new laptops, which are code-named K114 and K116, will be thinner and lighter than current versions and feature the next-generation M6 chips, according to the report, citing people familiar with the product. The new computers will utilize OLED technology, which is the same as that found in iPhones and iPad Pros.

Rumors of Apple developing a touch-screen MacBook Pro have been circulating for at least two years.

On Wednesday, Apple revealed its latest iPad Pro, MacBook Pro and Vision Pro, which are all powered by its M5 chip. These will be generally available on October 22.

It was also reported on Wednesday that Apple plans to expand its manufacturing operations in Vietnam to build the next generation of smart home devices, including a tabletop robot. The buzz is that a tabletop robot product for consumers could launch around 2027.

data.title: M&T Bank signals bottoming in CRE portfolio and targets $1.8B Q4 NII as loan growth momentum builds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: M&T Bank Corporation (MTB) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Daryl Bible, Senior EVP & CFO, opened by highlighting, "M&T continues to serve as a trusted partner for our customers and communities, bringing together people, capital and ideas to make a difference." He announced that M&T is now the top SBA lender across its footprint by total volume as of September 30, underscoring the strength of its small business enterprise.
  • Bible reported, "We produced strong returns with operating ROTA and ROTCE of 1.56% and 17.13%. Our net interest margin expanded to 3.68%, demonstrating our relatively neutral asset sensitivity, well-controlled deposit and funding costs and the continued benefit of fixed rate asset repricing."
  • Fee income, excluding notable items, reached a record level, and the third quarter efficiency ratio improved to 53.6%. Asset quality saw a $584 million or 7% reduction in commercialized criticized balances and a $61 million or 4% reduction in nonaccrual loans.
  • M&T increased its quarterly dividend per share by 11% to $1.50 and executed $409 million in share repurchases, while growing tangible book value per share by 3%.
  • Bible specifically noted, "Diluted GAAP earnings per share were $4.82, up from $4.24 in the prior quarter. Net income was $792 million compared to $716 million in the linked quarter."
  • He highlighted a notable $28 million fee item from an earn-out payment related to the 2023 CIT business sale, adding $0.14 to EPS.

Outlook

  • Bible stated, "We expect taxable equivalent NII of approximately $1.8 billion, which implies full year NII, excluding notable items, to be at the low end of the $7 billion to $7.15 billion range, in line with the outlook we discussed in September."
  • Fourth quarter net interest margin is expected to be about 3.7%. Management forecasts continued loan growth with average total loans of $137 billion to $138 billion and average deposits of $163 billion to $164 billion.
  • Noninterest income for the fourth quarter is projected at $670 million to $690 million, with full year noninterest income, excluding notable items, well above the top end of the prior $2.5 billion to $2.6 billion range.
  • Fourth quarter expenses, including intangible amortization, are expected to fall between $1.35 billion and $1.37 billion, with full year expense at the top half of the $5.4 billion to $5.5 billion range.
  • Net charge-offs for Q4 are expected to be 40 to 50 basis points, with a full year target of less than 40 basis points.

Financial Results

  • Operating ROTA and ROTCE were 1.56% and 17.13% respectively, with net interest margin at 3.68% for the quarter.
  • Average loans and leases increased to $136.5 billion, supported by higher commercial, residential mortgage, and consumer loans. CRE balances declined 4% to $24.3 billion.
  • Noninterest income was $752 million, up from $683 million in the linked quarter, with mortgage banking revenues at $147 million.
  • Noninterest expenses rose to $1.36 billion, reflecting increased salaries, severance, and other costs, while the efficiency ratio improved to 53.6%.
  • Net charge-offs for the quarter reached $146 million or 42 basis points, mainly due to specific C&I credits. Allowance for loan losses as a percent of total loans decreased to 1.58%.
  • CET1 ratio was an estimated 10.99%, unchanged from the prior quarter.

Q&A

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Asked about CRE inflection timing and magnitude. Bible responded, "The amount of production that's being done and that's going through our system and our approval rates are double what they were in prior quarters... we feel very optimistic on the growth of that coming in the next quarter or 2."
  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: Asked about increased CRE approvals. Bible explained, "That has eased up now... things are just flowing through a lot easier. And that's not just CRE. We're also seeing it on the C&I front."
  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Queried about NDFI exposure. Bible detailed, "We're probably 7% or 8% of total loans is what we have in that big bucket... we really focus on businesses in this bucket that we really believe in and are really good performing businesses."
  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked about CET1 target. Bible said, "We'll look for opportunities to potentially try to decrease our capital ratio down over time as that makes sense."
  • Matthew O'Connor, Deutsche Bank: Queried about credit environment and fraud-related commercial events. Bible responded, "There's definitely stress out there and sometimes people can only go so long and then they have to kind of throw in the towel."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive but pressed for clarity on CRE bottoming, capital strategy, and credit risk, particularly around NDFI and fraud-related losses.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently stating optimism about loan growth, CRE outlook, and risk controls. Bible used language such as "we feel very optimistic" and "we feel really good that it's going to bottom and start to grow."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive regarding CRE recovery and operating leverage, while analysts continued to probe for risk signals and sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management upgraded its discussion of CRE, signaling improving approval rates and production activity, whereas last quarter it remained cautious about the timing of CRE bottoming.
  • The outlook for net interest income, margin, and expense remained consistent with prior guidance, but Q3 results showed improved fee income and a higher dividend.
  • Analysts shifted focus from general caution on commercial and CRE loan growth to more specific questions about credit risk, NDFI exposure, and fraud events.
  • Management’s tone reflected growing confidence in the normalization of credit quality and the effectiveness of new processes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the potential impact of a weakening labor market, prolonged government shutdown, and economic slowdown on consumer spending and business CapEx.
  • Bible acknowledged, "We remain attuned to the risk of the slowdown in coming quarters due to the weakening labor market."
  • Analysts raised concerns about CRE portfolio inflection, credit exposure to NDFIs, and the impact of fraud-related events on commercial lending.
  • Management outlined measures to closely monitor and mitigate exposures, especially in higher-risk loan categories and operational risks.

Final Takeaway

M&T Bank delivered strong operating momentum in Q3 2025, underpinned by robust loan growth, improved fee income, and enhanced efficiency. Management signaled optimism that CRE balances are approaching a bottom, with approval rates and production activity rising. The bank reaffirmed guidance for Q4 NII at $1.8 billion and expects continued credit discipline, capital strength, and targeted growth across its footprint, while remaining vigilant to evolving economic and regulatory risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tech Voices: Oracle's gross margins, Meta deal; TikTok's algorithm
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) said its AI infrastructure projects can generate gross margins of around 35%.

At its Oracle AI World conference in Las Vegas on Thursday, Oracle used a six-year, $60 billion AI infrastructure project as an example of one that would achieve a gross margin of 35%, according to Bloomberg. 

Oracle signed a $300 billion, five-year AI cloud contract with OpenAI (OPENAI) in September. 

  • Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) also confirmed that it now has Meta (NASDAQ:META) as an AI infrastructure customer, according to CNBC. 

Oracle Co-CEO Clay Magouyrk told analysts at the company's Las Vegas event that in 30 days during the quarter, the company contracted $65 billion in new AI cloud infrastructure commitments, according to CNBC. 

“It was across seven different contracts from four different customers,” Magouyrk said. “None of those customers are OpenAI. I know some people are questioning sometimes, ’Hey, is it just OpenAI? The reality is, we think OpenAI is a great customer, but we have many customers.”

Magouyrk confirmed that Meta (NASDAQ:META) was one of the four customers. 

  • A leading congressman is questioning whether TikTok should be allowed to license its algorithm from China's ByteDance (BDNCE) after its U.S. assets are sold later this year.

"I think anytime you have [China] with leverage over the algorithm, I think that's a problem," said Rep. John Moolenaar (R-Mich.), chair of the House Select Committee on China, according to Reuters. 

"I just believe you have to have a new algorithm, and I don't know that you can reprogram," Moolenaar added. 

Last month, the Trump administration announced that TikTok's U.S. assets would be sold to a group of primarily American investors. As part of the deal, U.S. TikTok would license its algorithm from ByteDance. The algorithm would then be retrained and managed by Oracle (ORCL).  

ByteDance has until Jan. 20 to sell TikTok's U.S. assets or risk having the social media site shut down, Reuters added.

data.title: M&T Bank stock slides even after fee income drives Q3 earnings beat
data.description:
M&T Bank (NYSE:MTB) shares drifted down 4.3% in Thursday midafternoon trading even after the lender posted better-than-expected earnings and revenue for Q3 2025, buoyed in part by strong fee income.

The bank sees Q4 fee income of $670M-$690M (midpoint $680M) and average deposits of $163B-$164B (midpoint $163.3B).

Meanwhile, Q3 operating EPS of $4.82, vs. $4.56 average analyst estimate, rose from $4.28 in the prior quarter and $4.02 in Q3 2024.

Taxable-equivalent net interest income was $1.77B, up from $1.72B in Q2 and $1.74B in last year's Q3.

Noninterest income rose to $752M from $683M in the earlier quarter and $606M in Q3 2024.

Provision for credit losses was unchanged at $125M in Q3 vs. Q2, but drifted up from $120M in Q3 2024.

Average loans edged up to $136.5B from $135.4B in Q2. Average deposits slipped to $162.7B from $163.4B in Q2. 

“We see strong upside potential in mortgage banking given MTB's above-average exposure and built-up housing demand," said CFRA Research analyst Alexander Yokum. "The bank's disciplined pricing and margin strength position it well for continued operating leverage as funding costs stabilize at 2.71%.”

Note that MTB's dip comes as regional bank stocks largely retreated Thursday after the disclosures by two banks of irregularities related to some loans.

data.title: KeyCorp outlines path to 15%+ ROTCE by 2027 with accelerated share repurchases and NIM growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KeyCorp (KEY) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Christopher Gorman, Chairman, President & CEO, emphasized, "Our third quarter results reflect the steady progress we continue to make in achieving higher levels of both profitability and returns. We reported earnings per share of $0.41. Additionally, return on assets surpassed 1%. Pre-provision net revenue was up $33 million quarter-over-quarter or 5%, marking the sixth straight quarter of improving PPNR." He also stated, "We achieved a 2.75% NIM in the quarter, reaching our year-end target 1 quarter ahead of schedule." Gorman highlighted franchise momentum, with relationship households and commercial clients both growing at about 2% this year, and assets under management in wealth reaching a record $68 billion. He confirmed, "We remain on track to increase our frontline staff by approximately 10% this year." Gorman outlined medium-term targets: "We believe we can achieve a return on tangible common equity of 15% or better on a run rate basis by the end of 2027." He also announced plans for share buybacks: "Key expects to be back in the open market repurchasing approximately a $100 million of common stock in the fourth quarter."

  • Clark Khayat, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Revenue was up 17% year-over-year, while expenses increased 7%, tax equivalent net interest income was up 4% sequentially, primarily driven by commercial loan and low-cost client deposit growth." Khayat noted, "Tangible book value per share increased 4% sequentially and 14% year-over-year." He added, "We were pleased to have received a one notch upgrade to both our long and short-term ratings from Fitch this quarter with our senior unsecured debt now rated [A-]."

Outlook

  • The company now expects full year net interest income growth of about 22%, at the high end of the previously guided 20% to 22% range. Khayat explained, "Our fourth quarter exit rate NII should grow 13% or more compared to the fourth quarter of 2024. Assuming a fairly flat balance sheet in the fourth quarter compared to the third, this implies a fourth quarter NIM in the 2.75% to 2.8% range." Fees are expected to grow between 5% and 6%, with the company aiming for the higher end of the range if M&A pipelines pull through. Expenses for the full year are expected to fall within the middle of the approximately 4% range previously provided. The company remains on track to deliver its second best year in investment banking history and plans to continue share repurchases into 2026.

Financial Results

  • KeyCorp reported earnings per share of $0.41. Pre-provision net revenue increased by $33 million quarter-over-quarter, and net interest margin reached 2.75%. Revenue increased 17% year-over-year, and noninterest income was up 8%. Net charge-offs totaled $114 million, representing 42 basis points of average loans. Tangible book value per share rose 4% sequentially and 14% year-over-year. Average loans increased by $0.5 billion sequentially, led by commercial and industrial (C&I) loans. Deposit balances and average noninterest-bearing deposits both grew. Total deposit costs declined by 2 basis points. The CET1 ratio was 11.8% at quarter end. Commercial mortgage servicing fees remained near historic highs, and assets under management set a new record at $68 billion.

Q&A

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked for more detail on drivers to the 15%+ ROTCE target and why it can't be higher. Khayat responded that "the 15%, we see as low risk, no real big swings in here. So this is running our business effectively as we're doing today... if we took our current market capital at 10.3%, and we took that down to the peer level of 9.1%, that would generate an additional 2% of our ROTCE."
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA Securities: Inquired about Key’s approach to bank M&A and the financial risks. Gorman stated, "Specifically as it relates to bank M&A, that's pretty far down the capital priorities... it would have to check the box on a variety of important financial metrics, inclusive of tangible book value dilution."
  • Brian Foran, Truist Securities: Sought clarity on loan book composition and growth targets. Gorman said, "In the fourth quarter, we will basically accelerate our C&I book by about $1 billion."
  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about share repurchase pacing. Khayat replied, "$100 million for the fourth quarter is likely going to be the low level as we move forward, subject to the normal macro caveat movements."
  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Pressed on M&A pricing sensitivity. Gorman emphasized, "I am personally sensitive to tangible book value dilution."
  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked about operating leverage and efficiency ratio. Khayat explained, "We expect to fully deliver positive operating leverage every year... more NII given that shows up with a 0 efficiency ratio."
  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous: Inquired about deposit growth mix. Khayat described, "We also saw consumer come down a few basis points... we're seeing kind of static balances but better mix from our standpoint."
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Questioned expense growth from hiring. Gorman noted, "Our goal... was to grow by 10% the folks in our wealth business... as you look at our middle market, we've made huge progress."
  • Gerard Cassidy, RBC: Asked about regulatory changes. Gorman observed, "I've been really pleased with what has been a pretty dramatic change... just a refocusing on safety and soundness."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: Queried about investment banking verticals. Gorman indicated, "Where we're seeing just significant growth in terms of our backlogs are principally in the areas of energy... the other area... is health care."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed a neutral to slightly positive tone, pressing for details on capital return, M&A appetite, and loan growth but generally acknowledging management’s explanations. Concerns focused on M&A risks and capital deployment, but the Q&A included several acknowledgments of clarity and detail.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently restating disciplined capital management and strong business momentum. Phrases such as "we believe we can achieve" and "we are well positioned" appeared, while defensive responses were limited. Both the CEO and CFO addressed analyst concerns directly and reaffirmed the company’s commitment to organic growth and capital sensitivity.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive about capital return and medium-term targets, while analysts’ questions reflected ongoing skepticism about M&A but less concern about credit quality.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for net interest income growth increased from 20%-22% to about 22%. Fourth quarter NIM target remains at 2.75% to 2.8%. The share buyback plan ($100 million in Q4) is more definitive compared to last quarter’s "crawl, walk, run" language. The ROTCE medium-term goal of 15%+ was newly detailed, with a path to potentially higher levels through capital management. Commercial pipelines and AUM both saw new record levels this quarter, up from already strong positions in Q2. Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to greater confidence in operating momentum and capital deployment, while analysts continued to focus on capital allocation and M&A risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified no direct exposure to recent auto industry bankruptcies. Khayat noted expectations for commercial mortgage servicing fees to decline in Q4 due to lower Fed funds rates and resolutions in the servicing book. Ramani, Chief Risk Officer, described portfolio management controls for NDFI exposure, emphasizing "a very advanced limit structure that prevents outside growth." Analyst concerns centered on M&A risks, tangible book value dilution, and sensitivity to capital allocation timing.

Final Takeaway

KeyCorp’s third quarter call highlighted steady operating momentum, increased full-year guidance, and a clear path to a 15%+ ROTCE by the end of 2027, supported by disciplined capital management, ongoing loan and deposit growth, and robust performance in wealth and fee-based businesses. Management confirmed a $100 million share buyback in Q4 and signaled flexibility for further capital deployment, while reiterating that M&A remains a low priority unless strict strategic and financial criteria are met.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Benchmark U.S. 10-year yield falls below 4%, hits lowest level since early April
data.description:
U.S. Treasury yields slid on Thursday as investors shunned risky equities and flocked to the safety of bonds. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) fell below the key 4% level for the first time since early September, hitting its lowest level in seven months.

The US10Y was last down 6 basis points to a session low of 3.98%. It was on track to close below the 4% level for the first time since April 6, just a few days after U.S. President Donald Trump upended markets with his sweeping reciprocal tariffs announcement.

A resurgence in trade tensions between the U.S. and China last week has derailed Wall Street's record-breaking run. Traders have snapped up safe-haven assets such as bonds, driving yields lower, and precious metals like gold and silver. 

Weakness in the labor market along with pro-monetary policy easing commentary from Federal Reserve policymakers has also contributed to the bond-buying.

"In fixed-income, Treasuries are gaining, led by the short-end, as Fed Governors Waller and Miran have just claimed that imminent rate cuts are needed, although the former speaker prefers a cautious walk down the central bank’s stairs, while the latter wants to jump lower, favoring a half-point reduction," José Torres, senior economist at Interactive Brokers, said.

On Thursday, another catalyst driving market participants to the safety of bonds was a slump in shares of regional banks. The disclosure of bad loans by Zions Bancorporation (ZION) and Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL) sent ripples through the industry.

Aside from the US10Y, other maturities also slid across the board. The shorter-end, more rate-sensitive 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was last down 8 basis points to 3.43%, while the longer-end 30-year yield (US30Y) was down 5 basis points to 4.58%.

Here are some exchange-traded funds of interest that track the fixed-income markets: (NASDAQ:TLT), (NYSEARCA:TLH), (NASDAQ:IEF), (NASDAQ:IEI), (NASDAQ:SHY), (NYSEARCA:SGOV), (NYSEARCA:SCHO), (NYSEARCA:BIL), (NYSEARCA:AGG), (NASDAQ:BND), (NASDAQ:VCIT), (NYSEARCA:MUB), (NASDAQ:MBB), (NYSEARCA:JNK), (NYSEARCA:LQD), (NYSEARCA:HYG), and (NYSEARCA:TIP).
